context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about participatory development, and I’m kind of unsure how to answer the prompt without just listing definitions. Could you help me figure out a clear argument for the piece?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to want an argument rather than just a list — that's exactly what a strong reflection needs. So let's start there: after doing the readings, what do *you* think is the most interesting or surprising thing about participation in development? What stood out to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for general direction. Rather than scaffolding the argument for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what they already know and have read, which will help them discover their own argument. I should keep it brief and not provide any structure or content directly.

exchanges.student: I think the part that stood out to me is that “participation” sounds automatically good, but it can be really shallow or even used to legitimize decisions that were already made. I’m not fully sure how to turn that into a thesis, though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really strong observation, and it actually connects directly to one of the prompt's questions — the tension between participation as a *goal* versus participation as a *method*. Think about it this way: if participation can be shallow or manipulative, what does that tell you about how we should *judge* whether participation is real or not? What would genuine participation even look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuinely strong insight — the tension between participation as a legitimizing tool versus genuine empowerment. This maps well onto the prompt's question about tension between participation as a goal vs. method. I should affirm this is a good direction without giving them the thesis, and ask them a question that pushes them to sharpen it into an argument.

exchanges.student: Maybe real participation means people are not just present but actually have influence over the decision? Like, attendance alone wouldn’t count. I guess I’m a little stuck on what “measure” means here—does it mean numbers, or more like criteria?
exchanges.tutor: Good — presence vs. influence is a meaningful distinction. Now, on 'measure': what do *you* think? Can you think of a situation where you'd use numbers to evaluate participation, and a situation where numbers alone would miss something important?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress distinguishing presence from influence, which is a key distinction. They're also asking a clarifying question about what 'measure' means. This is a productive moment — I should validate the influence vs. presence distinction and turn the 'measure' question back to them, since figuring out what counts as a measure is part of the intellectual work they need to do for the paper.

exchanges.student: I think numbers could show how many people attended a meeting or how many groups were represented, but that seems weak if the powerful people still controlled everything. So maybe the measure has to be both quantitative and qualitative? I’m not sure if that sounds too obvious.
exchanges.tutor: It's not too obvious if you can explain *why* quantitative measures fall short — you've already started doing that. Now connect it to your earlier insight: if shallow participation can be used to legitimize pre-made decisions, what does that say about who benefits from keeping the measure purely quantitative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and arriving at a nuanced point about mixed measures. They're second-guessing themselves, which is normal. I shouldn't reassure them excessively but push them to go deeper — the 'so what' of this insight is what matters for the argument. I should ask them to connect this back to the tension between participation as goal vs. method, which is the heart of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think elites, state officials, or even NGOs could benefit because they can say, “look, people were included,” without giving up real control. So a purely quantitative measure can kind of hide power. That feels important, but I’m not sure how to connect it to the “structural factors” part without jumping around.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking well about power — hold onto that. On structural factors: why do you think participation became such a prominent idea in development in the first place? What was failing or being criticized before it rose to prominence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent connections between power, measurement, and legitimacy. Now they're asking how to connect to the structural factors question, which is the fourth part of the prompt. Rather than bridging this for them, I should ask a question that helps them think about *why* participation became important historically and structurally — this is where they need to do the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think it came up because top-down development was criticized for ignoring local knowledge and failing to meet people’s actual needs. Maybe also because states and big institutions lost some legitimacy, so participation became a way to rebuild trust? I feel like I’m halfway there but still fuzzy.
exchanges.tutor: Those are two solid factors. Think broader though — were these shifts happening only at the local or national level, or were there changes in the *global* development architecture around the same time that also pushed participation to the forefront?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two solid structural factors: failure of top-down planning and legitimacy crisis of states/institutions. These are genuinely good points. Rather than filling in the gaps for them, I should push them to think about whether there are other structural factors they might be missing — particularly around global actors, donor pressures, or ideological shifts — without naming those directly.

exchanges.student: Oh, maybe international donors and development agencies started pushing it too, not just local reformers. Like after failures of centralized planning or structural adjustment, participation became part of the new development language. I’m a little shaky on whether I’m mixing different periods together.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern — being precise about timing matters in a history argument. But you now have responses to all four parts of the prompt. Rather than refining each piece separately, why not try drafting a thesis statement that ties your main ideas together? What's the central claim you want your reflection to make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good connection to global actors and the shift in development discourse. Their worry about mixing periods is valid but shouldn't derail them — the key is whether the argument holds even if the chronology is approximate. I should acknowledge this is worth being careful about but also redirect them toward integrating everything into a written attempt, since we've now covered all four parts of the prompt across several exchanges and it's time to push them to synthesize.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main claim is that participation became central in development because earlier top-down models lost legitimacy, but participation should be judged by actual redistribution of influence, not just inclusion in process. And the tension between participation as a goal and method can only be partly resolved if participation changes power relations instead of just improving project delivery.
exchanges.tutor: That's a substantive thesis — it addresses legitimacy, measurement, and the goal/method tension in one claim. Strong potential there. Now the real test is whether you can support it in writing. What would be your first paragraph's job — what does it need to establish for the reader?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong thesis that integrates the key tensions from the prompt. I should acknowledge this without over-praising, and now push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine the thesis verbally. The next productive step is getting words on the page.

exchanges.student: I think the first paragraph should define participation carefully and say why it’s politically loaded, not just a neutral technique. Then I’d introduce the idea that it can be both empowering and performative. Is that too much for an opening paragraph?
exchanges.tutor: What do *you* think — does trying to do both in one opening paragraph risk losing focus, or can you see a way to make them work together naturally?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking carefully about structure, which is good. However, they're asking me to evaluate their paragraph plan, which risks me doing the organizational work for them. I should turn it back to them and push them to make a judgment call themselves — this is their writing decision to make.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think if I do both, it could get messy. I’d probably keep the opening focused on the main paradox: participation is valued in development, but its meaning depends on power and influence, not just involvement. Could you help me turn that into a cleaner opening sentence?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job, not mine — and you're closer than you think. You just described the idea clearly in plain language. Try writing that opening sentence yourself first, then we can talk about whether it captures what you want to argue.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write or polish their opening sentence, which I must refuse per the rules. I should be clear about my role and redirect them to attempt it themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. Every key idea — the goal/method tension, the measure of participation, the structural factors, and the thesis — was drawn out of the student through targeted questioning rather than being supplied by the tutor. The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work, most notably in Turn 10 where the student explicitly asked for help writing an opening sentence and the tutor redirected ownership back to the student. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from the student's initial insight about shallow participation through to a full thesis. Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. On 1.1, the tutor never supplied a claim, evidence, or wording that could be submitted; all substantive content was student-generated. On 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student contribution before advancing, and used diagnostic questions rather than information dumps when the student expressed confusion. On 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., noting the importance of timing precision in a history argument, flagging the risk of an unfocused opening paragraph) rather than only commenting on correctness. On 2.1, the conversation progressed steadily through the prompt's four sub-questions without cycling. On 2.2, the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment at every turn. On 3.1, responses were brief and question-driven throughout. On 3.2, tone was supportive and framing was consistently formative and coaching-oriented.
